title_en: The Obligation of Hajj and Its Wisdom
content_en:
Hajj is the fifth pillar of Islam, obligatory upon every Muslim adult who is physically capable of enduring the journey and financially able to bear its costs without leaving their dependents in hardship. This obligation is established beyond any doubt in the Quran, the Sunnah, and the ijma (scholarly consensus) of the Muslim Ummah. Allah commands in the Quran: 'And pilgrimage to the House is a duty owed to Allah by people who are able to undertake it' (3:97). The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) included Hajj in his foundational enumeration of the pillars of Islam, and the scholars are unanimous that denying the obligation of Hajj upon those who meet its conditions constitutes a departure from Islam.

Mufti Muhammad ibn Adam al-Kawthari's scholarly work on Hajj and Umrah is distinguished by its depth of jurisprudential analysis and its accessibility to the educated contemporary Muslim. Drawing on the Hanafi fiqh tradition while engaging extensively with the other three Sunni schools and with contemporary fiqh questions, Mufti ibn Adam provides a comprehensive treatment of the legal dimensions of Hajj that complements the spiritual guidance found in popular guides.

The wisdom (hikmah) behind the obligation of Hajj is multiple and profound. At the theological level, Hajj is the most complete expression of Islam's central principle of surrender to Allah: the pilgrim leaves home, wealth, family, and comfort, dons the simple equalizing garments of ihram, and places himself entirely at Allah's disposal. This total surrender — expressed in the talbiyah chant, 'Labbayk Allahumma Labbayk' (Here I am, O Allah, here I am) — is both the aspiration and the achievement of the sincere Hajj pilgrim.

At the social level, Hajj serves as the annual assembly of the global Muslim Ummah — a gathering of millions from every nation, language, ethnicity, and social class who stand together in equality before their Creator, wearing the same simple white garments, performing the same rites, and directing their hearts toward the same divine Being. The Hajj is the most powerful visible expression of the Islamic principle of human equality before Allah, and its impact on the social consciousness of those who experience it has been documented across the centuries. The African-American civil rights leader Malcolm X famously described how his Hajj experience transformed his understanding of racial equality and human brotherhood.

At the historical level, Hajj reconnects the Muslim to the foundational narrative of the Abrahamic tradition — the story of Ibrahim, Hajar, and Ismail — that forms the spiritual ancestry of the Muslim Ummah. Each rite of Hajj commemorates an event in this foundational narrative: the tawaf recalls Ibrahim's establishment of the Ka'bah as the house of worship; the sa'i recalls Hajar's search for water for Ismail; the sacrifice on the Day of Nahr recalls Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son in obedience to Allah's command. By performing these rites, the Hajj pilgrim literally re-enacts the founding stories of Islamic worship and renews their connection to the prophetic heritage.

The spiritual benefits of Hajj — its promise of complete forgiveness of past sins, its transformative effect on the pilgrim's character, and its renewal of the pilgrim's relationship with Allah — place it among the most powerful acts of worship in the Islamic tradition. The Prophet's promise that a properly performed Hajj returns its performer to the state of sinlessness at birth is one of the most encouraging statements in the entire hadith literature, and it motivates millions of Muslims to undertake the journey despite its considerable costs and difficulties.
content_id:
Haji adalah rukun Islam kelima, wajib atas setiap Muslim dewasa yang mampu secara fisik untuk menanggung perjalanan dan mampu secara finansial untuk menanggung biayanya tanpa meninggalkan tanggungan mereka dalam kesulitan. Kewajiban ini ditetapkan tanpa keraguan apapun dalam Al-Quran, Sunnah, dan ijma (konsensus ulama) umat Muslim. Allah memerintahkan dalam Al-Quran: 'Dan mengerjakan haji adalah kewajiban manusia terhadap Allah, yaitu bagi orang yang mampu mengadakan perjalanan ke Baitullah' (3:97). Nabi Muhammad ﷺ memasukkan haji dalam enumerasi dasar rukun Islam, dan para ulama sepakat bahwa mengingkari kewajiban haji atas mereka yang memenuhi syaratnya merupakan penyimpangan dari Islam.

Karya ilmiah Mufti Muhammad ibn Adam al-Kawthari tentang haji dan umrah dibedakan oleh kedalaman analisis jurisprudensialnya dan aksesibilitasnya bagi Muslim kontemporer yang berpendidikan. Mengambil dari tradisi fiqh Hanafi sambil terlibat secara luas dengan tiga madzhab Sunni lainnya dan dengan pertanyaan-pertanyaan fiqh kontemporer, Mufti ibn Adam memberikan pembahasan komprehensif tentang dimensi hukum haji yang melengkapi panduan spiritual yang ditemukan dalam panduan-panduan populer.

Hikmah di balik kewajiban haji bersifat ganda dan mendalam. Pada tingkat teologis, haji adalah ekspresi paling lengkap dari prinsip sentral Islam tentang penyerahan diri kepada Allah: jemaah haji meninggalkan rumah, kekayaan, keluarga, dan kenyamanan, mengenakan pakaian ihram yang sederhana dan menyetarakan semua orang, dan menempatkan dirinya sepenuhnya dalam ketentuan Allah. Penyerahan total ini — yang diekspresikan dalam talbiyah, 'Labbayk Allahumma Labbayk' (Aku datang memenuhi panggilan-Mu, ya Allah) — adalah sekaligus aspirasi dan pencapaian jemaah haji yang tulus.

Pada tingkat sosial, haji berfungsi sebagai majelis tahunan umat Muslim global — kumpulan jutaan orang dari setiap bangsa, bahasa, etnis, dan kelas sosial yang berdiri bersama dalam kesetaraan di hadapan Pencipta mereka, mengenakan pakaian putih sederhana yang sama, melakukan ritual-ritual yang sama, dan mengarahkan hati mereka kepada Dzat ilahi yang sama. Haji adalah ekspresi yang paling kuat dan nyata dari prinsip Islam tentang kesetaraan manusia di hadapan Allah, dan dampaknya pada kesadaran sosial mereka yang mengalaminya telah didokumentasikan sepanjang berabad-abad. Pemimpin hak-hak sipil Afrika-Amerika Malcolm X terkenal menggambarkan bagaimana pengalaman hajinya mengubah pemahamannya tentang kesetaraan ras dan persaudaraan manusia.

Pada tingkat historis, haji menghubungkan kembali Muslim dengan narasi fondasi tradisi Abrahami — kisah Ibrahim, Hajar, dan Ismail — yang membentuk leluhur spiritual umat Muslim. Setiap ritual haji memperingati sebuah peristiwa dalam narasi fondasi ini: tawaf mengingatkan penetapan Ibrahim atas Ka'bah sebagai rumah ibadah; sa'i mengingatkan pencarian Hajar akan air untuk Ismail; kurban pada Hari Nahr mengingatkan kesediaan Ibrahim untuk mengorbankan putranya dalam ketaatan kepada perintah Allah. Dengan melakukan ritual-ritual ini, jemaah haji secara harfiah memperagakan kembali kisah-kisah pendirian ibadah Islam dan memperbarui hubungan mereka dengan warisan kenabian.

Manfaat spiritual haji — janjinya atas pengampunan penuh dosa-dosa masa lalu, efek transformatifnya pada karakter jemaah haji, dan pembaruan hubungan jemaah haji dengan Allah — menempatkannya di antara amalan ibadah yang paling kuat dalam tradisi Islam. Janji Nabi ﷺ bahwa haji yang dilaksanakan dengan benar mengembalikan pelaksananya ke keadaan tanpa dosa seperti saat lahir adalah salah satu pernyataan paling menggembirakan dalam seluruh literatur hadis, dan hal itu memotivasi jutaan Muslim untuk melakukan perjalanan tersebut meskipun biaya dan kesulitannya yang cukup besar.
content_ar:
Hajj is the fifth pillar of Islam, obligatory upon every Muslim adult who is physically capable of enduring the journey and financially able to bear its costs without leaving their dependents in hardship. This obligation is established beyond any doubt in the القرآن الكريم, the السنة النبوية, and the ijma (scholarly consensus) of the Muslim Ummah. الله commands in the القرآن الكريم: 'And pilgrimage to the House is a duty owed to الله by people who are able to undertake it' (3:97). The النبي محمد ﷺ (صلى الله عليه وسلم) included Hajj in his foundational enumeration of the pillars of Islam, and the scholars are unanimous that denying the obligation of Hajj upon those who meet its conditions constitutes a departure from Islam.

Mufti Muhammad ibn Adam al-Kawthari's scholarly work on Hajj and Umrah is distinguished by its depth of jurisprudential analysis and its accessibility to the educated contemporary Muslim. Drawing on the Hanafi الفقه tradition while engaging extensively with the other three Sunni schools and with contemporary الفقه questions, Mufti ibn Adam provides a comprehensive treatment of the legal dimensions of Hajj that complements the spiritual guidance found in popular guides.

The wisdom (hikmah) behind the obligation of Hajj is multiple and profound. At the theological level, Hajj is the most complete expression of Islam's central principle of surrender to الله: the pilgrim leaves home, wealth, family, and comfort, dons the simple equalizing garments of ihram, and places himself entirely at الله's disposal. This total surrender — expressed in the talbiyah chant, 'Labbayk Allahumma Labbayk' (Here I am, O الله, here I am) — is both the aspiration and the achievement of the sincere Hajj pilgrim.

At the social level, Hajj serves as the annual assembly of the global Muslim Ummah — a gathering of millions from every nation, language, ethnicity, and social class who stand together in equality before their Creator, wearing the same simple white garments, performing the same rites, and directing their hearts toward the same divine Being. The Hajj is the most powerful visible expression of the Islamic principle of human equality before الله, and its impact on the social consciousness of those who experience it has been documented across the centuries. The African-American civil rights leader Malcolm X famously described how his Hajj experience transformed his understanding of racial equality and human brotherhood.

At the historical level, Hajj reconnects the Muslim to the foundational narrative of the Abrahamic tradition — the story of Ibrahim, Hajar, and Ismail — that forms the spiritual ancestry of the Muslim Ummah. Each rite of Hajj commemorates an event in this foundational narrative: the tawaf recalls Ibrahim's establishment of the Ka'bah as the house of worship; the sa'i recalls Hajar's search for water for Ismail; the sacrifice on the Day of Nahr recalls Ibrahim's willingness to sacrifice his son in obedience to الله's command. By performing these rites, the Hajj pilgrim literally re-enacts the founding stories of Islamic worship and renews their connection to the prophetic heritage.

The spiritual benefits of Hajj — its promise of complete forgiveness of past sins, its transformative effect on the pilgrim's character, and its renewal of the pilgrim's relationship with الله — place it among the most powerful acts of worship in the Islamic tradition. النبي ﷺ's promise that a properly performed Hajj returns its performer to the state of sinlessness at birth is one of the most encouraging statements in the entire الحديث literature, and it motivates millions of Muslims to undertake the journey despite its considerable costs and difficulties.
